column1: Term,"definition"
[cite_start]"--- CHAPTER 1: PHYSICAL QUANTITIES, UNITS AND MEASUREMENTS [cite: 3] ---",""
Physical quantity,"A quantity that can be measured. [cite_start]It consists of a numerical magnitude and a unit. [cite: 4]"
[cite_start]"Oscillation","Each complete to-and-fro motion is one oscillation. [cite: 5]"
[cite_start]"Period of a simple pendulum","The time taken for one complete oscillation. [cite: 6]"
[cite_start]"Scalar quantities","Physical quantities that have only magnitude. [cite: 7]"
[cite_start]"Vector quantities","Physical quantities that have both magnitude and direction. [cite: 8]"
[cite_start]"Speed","The distance moved per unit time. [cite: 9]"
[cite_start]"Velocity","The rate of change of displacement. [cite: 13]"
[cite_start]"--- CHAPTER 2: KINEMATICS [cite: 17] ---",""
[cite_start]"Average speed","Assumes that an object travels at the same speed throughout the entire distance. [cite: 18]"
[cite_start]"Average velocity","Assumes that an object travels at the same velocity throughout the entire displacement. [cite: 21]"
[cite_start]"Acceleration","The rate of change of velocity. [cite: 25]"
[cite_start]"Uniform acceleration","A constant rate of change of velocity. [cite: 28]"
[cite_start]"--- CHAPTER 3: DYNAMICS I: MASS AND WEIGHT [cite: 42] ---",""
[cite_start]"Mass","A measure of the amount of matter in a body. [cite: 43]"
[cite_start]"Weight","The gravitational force acting on an object that has mass. [cite: 44]"
[cite_start]"Gravitational field","A region in which a mass experiences a force due to gravitational attraction. [cite: 48]"
[cite_start]"Gravitational field strength","Defined as the gravitational force per unit mass placed at that point. [cite: 49]"
[cite_start]"--- CHAPTER 4: DYNAMICS II: FORCES [cite: 50] ---",""
[cite_start]"Newton's First Law of Motion","States that every object will continue in its state of rest or uniform motion in a straight line unless a resultant force acts on it. [cite: 51]"
[cite_start]"Inertia","Refers to the reluctance of the object to change its state of rest or motion, due to its mass. [cite: 52]"
[cite_start]"Newton's Second Law of Motion","States that when a resultant force acts on an object of a constant mass, the object will accelerate in the direction of the resultant force. [cite: 53]"
[cite_start]"Newton's Third Law of Motion","States that if body A exerts a force on body B, then body B will exert an equal and opposite force on body A. [cite: 55]"
[cite_start]"Friction","The contact force that opposes or tends to oppose motion between surfaces in contact. [cite: 56]"
[cite_start]"Terminal velocity","When the air resistance acting against an object equals its weight, the object starts to travel at a constant speed known as terminal velocity. [cite: 57]"
[cite_start]"--- CHAPTER 5: TURNING EFFECTS OF FORCES [cite: 58] ---",""

[cite_start]"Moment of a force","The moment of a force, or torque, about a pivot is the product of the force and the perpendicular distance from the pivot to the line of action of the force. [cite: 59]"

[cite_start]"Principle of moments","States that when a body is in equilibrium, the sum of clockwise moments about a pivot is equal to the sum of anticlockwise moments about the same pivot. [cite: 64]"
[cite_start]"Centre of gravity","An imaginary point where the entire weight of the object seems to act. [cite: 68]"
[cite_start]"Stability","A measure of its ability to maintain its original position. [cite: 69]"
[cite_start]"--- CHAPTER 6: PRESSURE [cite: 72] ---",""
[cite_start]"Pressure","The force acting per unit area. [cite: 73]"
[cite_start]"Density","Defined as mass per unit volume. [cite: 81]"
[cite_start]"Pascal's principle (or Pascal's law)","If a pressure is applied to an enclosed liquid, the pressure is transmitted to all other parts of the liquid undiminished. [cite: 86, 87]"
[cite_start]"--- CHAPTER 7: ENERGY [cite: 91] ---",""

[cite_start]"Principle of conservation of energy","States that energy cannot be created or destroyed. [cite: 98] [cite_start]It can be transferred from one energy store to another during an event or process. [cite: 99]"
[cite_start]"Work done by a constant force","The product of the force and the distance moved by the object in the direction of the force. [cite: 101]"
[cite_start]"Power","Defined as the work done or energy transferred per unit time. [cite: 107]"
[cite_start]"--- CHAPTER 8: KINETIC PARTICLE MODEL OF MATTER [cite: 115, 116] ---",""
[cite_start]"Kinetic particle model of matter","Is made up of tiny particles that are in continuous motion. [cite: 117]"
[cite_start]"Brownian motion","The random motion of particles in a fluid. [cite: 118, 119]"
[cite_start]"--- CHAPTER 9: THERMAL PROCESSES [cite: 122] ---",""
[cite_start]"Thermal equilibrium","Describes a state in which two or more objects have the same temperature and that there is no net transfer of energy between them. [cite: 123]"
[cite_start]"Conduction","A process of energy transfer where energy is transferred through the passing on of vibrational motion from one particle to another. [cite: 124]"
[cite_start]"Convection","A process of energy transfer by means of convection currents of a fluid (liquid or gas), due to a difference in density. [cite: 125]"
Radiation,"The process of energy transfer by electromagnetic waves. [cite_start]It does not require a medium. [cite: 126]"
[cite_start]"--- CHAPTER 10: THERMAL PROPERTIES OF MATTER [cite: 127] ---",""
[cite_start]"Internal energy","Consists of the kinetic energy associated with the random motion of the particles and the total potential energy between the particles in the system. [cite: 128]"
[cite_start]"Heat capacity","The change of its internal energy per unit change in its temperature. [cite: 129]"
[cite_start]"Specific heat capacity","The change of its internal energy per unit mass for each unit change in its temperature. [cite: 136]"
[cite_start]"Latent heat","The energy released or absorbed to change the state of a substance, at constant temperature. [cite: 145]"
[cite_start]"Latent heat of fusion","The amount of energy transferred to change a substance between the solid and liquid states, at constant temperature. [cite: 146]"
[cite_start]"Specific latent heat of fusion","The amount of energy transferred per unit mass of a substance to change between the solid and liquid states, at constant temperature. [cite: 147]"
[cite_start]"Latent heat of vaporisation","The amount of energy transferred to change a substance between the liquid and gaseous states, at constant temperature. [cite: 151]"

[cite_start]"Specific latent heat of vaporisation","The amount of energy transferred per unit mass of a substance to change it between the liquid and gaseous states, at constant temperature. [cite: 152]"
[cite_start]"--- CHAPTER 11: GENERAL WAVE PROPERTIES I: INTRODUCTION [cite: 158] ---",""
[cite_start]"Wave","A disturbance that propagates through space, carrying energy with it but not matter. [cite: 159]"
[cite_start]"Vibrations or oscillations","The periodic and repetitive motion of any selected point in a wave. [cite: 160]"
[cite_start]"Transverse wave","Has a direction of vibration that is perpendicular to the direction of wave travel. [cite: 161]"
[cite_start]"Longitudinal wave","Has a direction of vibration that is parallel to the direction of wave travel. [cite: 162]"
[cite_start]"Displacement","A vector pointing from its rest position to the point. [cite: 163]"
[cite_start]"Amplitude","Its maximum magnitude of displacement from its rest position. [cite: 164]"
Crests and Troughs,"Crests are the highest points of a transverse wave. [cite_start]Troughs are the lowest points of a transverse wave. [cite: 165]"
[cite_start]"In phase","Describes two points on a wave that always have the same direction of motion (moving in step). [cite: 166]"
[cite_start]"Wavelength","The shortest distance between two points which are in phase along the wave's direction. [cite: 167]"
[cite_start]"Period","The time taken by each point on the wave to complete one oscillation. [cite: 168]"
[cite_start]"Frequency","The number of oscillations each point completes per second. [cite: 169]"
[cite_start]"Wave speed","The distance travelled by a wave per second. [cite: 173]"
[cite_start]"Wavefront","An imaginary line joining all adjacent points that are in phase. [cite: 179]"
[cite_start]"--- CHAPTER 12: GENERAL WAVE PROPERTIES II: SOUND [cite: 180, 181] ---",""
[cite_start]"Sound","A longitudinal wave travelling in the air, created by a vibrating source. [cite: 182]"
[cite_start]"Infrasound","Frequencies below 20 Hz. [cite: 187]"
[cite_start]"Ultrasound","Frequencies above 20 000 Hz. [cite: 187]"
[cite_start]"Echo","The repetition of a sound due to the reflection of sound. [cite: 188]"
[cite_start]"Echolocation","The location of objects using reflected sound. [cite: 189]"
[cite_start]"--- CHAPTER 13: ELECTROMAGNETIC WAVES [cite: 191] ---",""
[cite_start]"Ionising waves","Those with higher frequencies and hence higher energies. [cite: 193]"
[cite_start]"--- CHAPTER 14: LIGHT [cite: 202] ---",""
[cite_start]"First law of reflection","States that the incident ray, reflected ray and the normal at the point of incidence lie in the same plane. [cite: 203]"
[cite_start]"Second law of reflection","States that the angle of incidence is equal to the angle of reflection. [cite: 204]"
[cite_start]"Refraction","The bending of light as light passes from one optical medium to another. [cite: 205]"
[cite_start]"First law of refraction","States that the incident ray, refracted ray and the normal at the point of incidence all lie in the same plane. [cite: 206]"
[cite_start]"Second law of refraction","States that for two given media, the ratio of the sine of angle of incidence to the sine of the angle of refraction is a constant. [cite: 207]"
[cite_start]"Refractive index","Defined as the ratio of the speed of light in a vacuum to the speed of light in that medium. [cite: 210]"

[cite_start]"Principle of reversibility of light rays","States that regardless of how many times a light ray has been reflected or refracted, it will follow the same path when its direction is reversed. [cite: 214]"
[cite_start]"Critical angle","Defined as the angle of incidence in an optically denser medium for which the angle of refraction in the less dense medium is 90 degrees. [cite: 215]"
[cite_start]"Total internal reflection","The complete reflection of a light ray in an optically denser medium at the boundary with an optically less dense medium. [cite: 216]"
[cite_start]"Focal length","The distance between the optical centre and the principal focus point. [cite: 219]"
[cite_start]"--- CHAPTER 15: STATIC ELECTRICITY [cite: 221] ---",""
[cite_start]"Electric field","A region in which an electric charge experiences an electric force. [cite: 223]"
[cite_start]"--- CHAPTER 16: CURRENT OF ELECTRICITY [cite: 224] ---",""
[cite_start]"Electric current","The rate of flow of charge with respect to time. [cite: 225]"
[cite_start]"Conventional current","The direction of flow of positive charges is described as the conventional current. [cite: 230]"
[cite_start]"Electromotive force (e.m.f)","The work done by the source in driving a unit charge around a complete circuit. [cite: 231]"
[cite_start]"Potential difference (p.d.)","The work done per unit charge in driving charges through the component and that it is measured in volts. [cite: 239]"
[cite_start]"Resistance","The ratio of the potential difference across it to the current flowing through it. [cite: 247]"
[cite_start]"--- CHAPTER 17: D.C. CIRCUITS [cite: 257] ---",""
[cite_start]"Potential divider","A voltage divider, which makes use of voltage drop across resistors in series to divide voltage. [cite: 268]"
[cite_start]"Transducers","Convert changes in the physical condition, such as pressure, temperature or light into electrical signals so that these physical conditions can be measured. [cite: 269]"
[cite_start]"--- CHAPTER 19: MAGNETISM [cite: 277] ---",""
[cite_start]"Induction (induced magnetism)","Can take place when a ferromagnetic material is placed close to a strong magnet or within a current-carrying solenoid. [cite: 278]"
[cite_start]"Magnetic field","A region in which the force of magnetism acts. [cite: 279]"
[cite_start]"Temporary magnets","Magnets that retain their magnetism in the presence of an electric current or a permanent magnetic field. [cite: 280]"
[cite_start]"Permanent magnets","Do not require the presence of an electric current or a permanent magnetic field to retain their magnetism. [cite: 281]"
[cite_start]"--- CHAPTER 20: ELECTROMAGNETISM [cite: 282] ---",""

[cite_start]"Motor effect","A current-carrying conductor will experience a force if the magnetic field applied is perpendicular (or has a component that is perpendicular) to the direction of flow of current. [cite: 287, 288]"
[cite_start]"--- CHAPTER 21: ELECTROMAGNETIC INDUCTION [cite: 295] ---",""
[cite_start]"Electromagnetic induction","The process through which an induced e.m.f. is produced in a conductor due to a changing magnetic field. [cite: 295, 296]"

[cite_start]"Faraday's Law of electromagnetic induction","States that the magnitude of the induced e.m.f. in a circuit is directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux in the circuit. [cite: 297, 298]"
[cite_start]"Magnetic flux","The magnetic field in a given area. [cite: 299]"

[cite_start]"Lenz's Law","States that the direction of the induced e.m.f. and hence the induced current in a closed circuit, is always such that its magnetic effect opposes the motion or change producing it. [cite: 299, 300]"
[cite_start]"Transformer","A device used in the transmission of electricity. [cite: 302]"
[cite_start]"--- CHAPTER 22: RADIOACTIVITY [cite: 311] ---",""
[cite_start]"Proton number (atomic number)","The number of protons in an atom. [cite: 312, 313]"
[cite_start]"Nucleon number (mass number)","The total number of neutrons and protons in the nucleus of an atom. [cite: 314, 315]"
[cite_start]"Isotopes","Atoms of the same element that have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons. [cite: 317]"
[cite_start]"Nuclear decay","A random process by which an unstable atomic nucleus loses its energy by emission of electromagnetic radiation or particle(s). [cite: 318, 319]"
[cite_start]"Ionising radiation","Radiation with high energies that can knock off electrons from atoms to form ions. [cite: 321]"
[cite_start]"Background radiation","Refers to nuclear radiation in an environment where no radioactive source has been deliberately introduced. [cite: 322]"
[cite_start]"Half-life","The time taken for half the nuclei of that nuclide in any sample to decay. [cite: 323]"
[cite_start]"Nuclear fission","A process in which the nucleus of an atom splits (usually into two parts) and releases a huge amount of energy. [cite: 324]"
[cite_start]"Nuclear fusion","A process in which two light atomic nuclei combine to form one heavier atomic nucleus and releases a huge amount of energy. [cite: 325]"
